docs.title: django-requests-api
docs.text: Small Django app: a requests.Session wrapper with a fixed base URL, plus helpers for language codes and query strings. Use it when several views talk to the same HTTP API and you want connection reuse and predictable URL joining.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.8 or newer
  • Django 4.2 or newer (see the package metadata for the exact matrix)

We test against current patch releases of supported Python and Django versions.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install django-requests-api

Add the app to INSTALLED_APPS:

INSTALLED_APPS = (
    # ...
    "requests_api",
    # ...
)

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
from requests_api import RequestsApi

client = RequestsApi("https://api.publicapis.org")
r = client.get("/entries")
print(r.json())

github = RequestsApi(
    "https://api.github.com",
    headers={"Authorization": "token abcdef"},
)
r = github.get("/user", headers={"Accept": "application/json"})
print(r.text)

Relative paths are joined to base_url with a single slash. Paths that already start with http:// or https:// are left as-is. The library depends on the requests package.

docs.title: Helpers
docs.text:
RequestsApi is defined in requests_api.requests_api. Shared utilities live in requests_api.helpers and are re-exported from the top-level package:

from requests_api import (
    copy_get_params_with_overrides,
    normalize_api_language,
    requests_api_for_base,
)

lang = normalize_api_language(
    request.LANGUAGE_CODE,
    allowed=("it", "en"),
    fallback="en",
)
params = copy_get_params_with_overrides(request, lang=lang)
client = requests_api_for_base("https://www.example.com")
r = client.get("api/resource", params=params, timeout=30)

requests_api_for_base returns one client per distinct base URL inside the process, up to a configurable limit (see Configuration).

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Optional settings (cache size for requests_api_for_base, optional default timeout helper) are read through requests_api.conf: Django settings REQUESTS_API_*, then APP_CONFIG["requests_api"], then requests_api.defaults. Details: Configuration.

docs.title: Example project
docs.text:
The DLRSP/example repo has a django-requests-api branch with a runnable demo. Clone steps: Example project.

There is also a Heroku demo (if still deployed).

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Clone the repo, run the test suite, open a PR: Contributing.

docs.title: References
docs.text: Links to requests, Django settings, other packages that ship this client, and an HTTP status code cheat sheet: References.

docs.title: Security
docs.text: Do not open security issues in public trackers. Email dlrsp.issue@gmail.com with a description; maintainers will coordinate a fix before public disclosure.

docs.title: Issues and discussions
docs.text:
• Bug reports and feature ideas: GitHub issues.
  • Usage questions: GitHub discussions if enabled, otherwise open an issue and label it as support.

Please search existing issues first and mention the package version, Python, and Django you use.

docs.title: Development setup
docs.text:
Fork django-requests-api, clone your fork, then:

python -m venv env
source env/bin/activate
# Windows: env\Scripts\activate

pip install -r requirements/py312-django42.txt
# Or another requirements/*.txt line that matches your environment.

pre-commit install

Style checks run via pre-commit on CI; keep changes PEP 8–friendly.

docs.title: Tests
docs.text:
From the repository root:

python runtests.py
python runtests.py -q
python runtests.py tests.test_conf

With pytest directly (uses tests.settings from pyproject.toml):

pytest tests/

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
Docs are Markdown under docs/. Build locally:

pip install mkdocs mkdocs-material
mkdocs serve

Write in clear English; short paragraphs beat long essays.

docs.title: Pull requests
docs.text: Open a PR from a branch (not main/master). Run the test suite before you push. If you add behavior, add or extend tests.

docs.title: Code of conduct
docs.text: Stay polite and professional. For broader norms, see the Django code of conduct.

docs.title: HTTP status codes (quick reference)
docs.text:
When you call remote APIs with RequestsApi, inspect response.status_code (integer) or use response.raise_for_status() from requests for 4xx/5xx.

This page is a compact cheat sheet. Authoritative definitions are in RFC 9110 and the MDN HTTP status reference.

docs.title: 1xx — Informational
docs.text:
Rare in typical requests usage; often handled by the HTTP stack before you see a final response.

Code Name Meaning 100 Continue Client may send the rest of the request body. 101 Switching Protocols Protocol upgrade (for example WebSocket). 103 Early Hints Hints while the final response is prepared.

docs.title: 2xx — Success
docs.text: Code Name Meaning 200 OK Request succeeded; meaning depends on method (GET: resource returned, etc.). 201 Created Resource created; often includes Location. 202 Accepted Accepted for processing, not completed yet. 204 No Content Success with an empty body. 206 Partial Content Part of a resource (range requests).

docs.title: 3xx — Redirection
docs.text:
Follow redirects automatically unless you pass allow_redirects=False to requests.

Code Name Meaning 301 Moved Permanently Resource has a new permanent URI. 302 Found Temporary redirect (historically “moved temporarily”). 303 See Other GET the URI in Location. 304 Not Modified Cached version still valid (conditional GET). 307 Temporary Redirect Repeat request to Location with the same method. 308 Permanent Redirect Like 301 but method must not change.

docs.title: 4xx — Client errors
docs.text:
The server understood the request but refuses or cannot satisfy it because of the client (auth, validation, wrong URL, etc.).

Code Name Meaning 400 Bad Request Malformed syntax or invalid message framing. 401 Unauthorized Authentication required or failed. 402 Payment Required Reserved; rarely used in practice. 403 Forbidden Authenticated but not allowed to access the resource. 404 Not Found No matching resource for the URI. 405 Method Not Allowed HTTP method not supported for this resource. 406 Not Acceptable Cannot produce a response matching Accept. 408 Request Timeout Server gave up waiting for the full request. 409 Conflict Conflict with current state (for example duplicate create). 410 Gone Resource existed but is permanently gone. 413 Payload Too Large Request body too large. 414 URI Too Long Request URI longer than the server accepts. 415 Unsupported Media Type Body media type not supported. 422 Unprocessable Entity Common in JSON APIs: validation failed (WebDAV / API convention). 429 Too Many Requests Rate limited; often includes Retry-After.

docs.title: 5xx — Server errors
docs.text:
The server failed to fulfill an apparently valid request.

Code Name Meaning 500 Internal Server Error Generic server-side failure. 501 Not Implemented Server does not support the functionality. 502 Bad Gateway Upstream server returned an invalid response. 503 Service Unavailable Temporarily overloaded or down; may include Retry-After. 504 Gateway Timeout Upstream did not respond in time.

docs.title: Using this with RequestsApi
docs.text:
from requests import HTTPError

client = RequestsApi("https://api.example.com")
r = client.get("v1/items")
try:
    r.raise_for_status()
except HTTPError:
    # r.status_code tells you which row above applies
    raise
data = r.json()

For APIs that return errors in JSON bodies with HTTP 200, you still need to read the payload—status codes alone are not enough.

docs.title: Core stack
docs.text:
• requests — HTTP library used under the hood.
  • Django settings — where APP_CONFIG and REQUESTS_API_* live.
  • HTTP status codes — short reference when handling response.status_code.

docs.title: Other Django / Python packages
docs.text: Any project can depend on django-requests-api; you only need requests_api in INSTALLED_APPS and a normal install from PyPI or VCS.

docs.title: Known open-source consumers (GitHub)
docs.text:
These repositories under github.com/DLRSP are discovered with GitHub code search. Queries and exclusions live in .github/used-in.yaml. The table between the HTML markers is refreshed automatically by the reusable update-used-in workflow (weekly on this repository).

Repository Role django-iubenda Django application for privacy and cookies policy managed by Iubenda.

The example repo is the shared demo workspace; use the branch named after the package you are trying. It may not appear in the table until the search index picks up matching files.

If a public consumer is missing, extend the markers in README.md / references.md only when you change the manual text around them—otherwise wait for the next scheduled sync or open a PR that updates the generated block.

Private sites and forks are expected to use django-requests-api as well; they are not listed here.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Optional behavior is controlled through requests_api.conf. Resolution order matches other DLRSP Django apps (for example django-iubenda, copyai):

  1. Top-level Django settings whose names start with REQUESTS_API_ (when set).
  2. settings.APP_CONFIG["requests_api"] — a dict of short keys (see table).
  3. Defaults in requests_api.defaults.
from requests_api import (
    get_requests_api_cached_clients_maxsize,
    get_requests_api_default_request_timeout,
)

docs.title: Keys under APP_CONFIG["requests_api"]
docs.text: Key Django setting Meaning CACHED_CLIENTS_MAXSIZE REQUESTS_API_CACHED_CLIENTS_MAXSIZE Upper bound on how many different base URLs requests_api_for_base keeps in memory. Default 8. If 0 or negative, no caching: every call builds a new RequestsApi. DEFAULT_REQUEST_TIMEOUT REQUESTS_API_DEFAULT_REQUEST_TIMEOUT Seconds, or omit. Default is None. The package does not add timeout= to HTTP calls by itself; use get_requests_api_default_request_timeout() when you want one place to read a project-wide default and pass it into get / post / etc.

docs.title: settings.py example
docs.text:
APP_CONFIG = {
    "requests_api": {
        "CACHED_CLIENTS_MAXSIZE": 16,
        "DEFAULT_REQUEST_TIMEOUT": 30.0,
    },
}

REQUESTS_API_CACHED_CLIENTS_MAXSIZE = 4  # wins over APP_CONFIG for this key only

docs.title: Tests
docs.text: If you toggle cache-related settings between tests, call clear_requests_api_client_cache() (exported from requests_api) so the next requests_api_for_base call does not see stale instances.

docs.title: Further reading
docs.text:
• Home — install and API overview.
  • Example project — clone and run the demo branch.

docs.title: Example project
docs.text: The demo lives in DLRSP/example on branch django-requests-api. It is a small Django site wired to this package.

docs.title: Clone and run
docs.text:
git clone --depth=50 --branch=django-requests-api https://github.com/DLRSP/example.git example-requests-api
cd example-requests-api

python -m venv env
source env/bin/activate
# Windows: env\Scripts\activate

pip install -r requirements/py312-django42.txt
# Pick another file under requirements/ if it matches your Python/Django versions.

python manage.py migrate
python manage.py runserver

Open http://127.0.0.1:8000 and browse the routes the example defines.

To use only this library in an existing project, you do not need the example: install django-requests-api, add requests_api to INSTALLED_APPS, and import from requests_api as in the home page.
